note_data.title: Differentiation
keyPoints: **Differentiation** is the process of finding the gradient function dy/dx from the equation of a curve
The **gradient** at any point on a curve is given by the value of dy/dx at that point
The equation of the **tangent** to a curve at point (a, b) is y - b = m(x - a) where m = dy/dx at x = a
The equation of the **normal** to a curve at point (a, b) is y - b = (-1/m)(x - a) where m = dy/dx at x = a
At **stationary points**, dy/dx = 0 and the gradient of the tangent is zero
Use the **chain rule** dy/dx = dy/du × du/dx when differentiating composite functions
**Implicit differentiation** is used when y cannot be expressed explicitly in terms of x

definitions.definition: The derivative dy/dx which gives the gradient of the tangent to the curve at any point
A point on a curve where dy/dx = 0 and the tangent is horizontal
A method for differentiating composite functions where dy/dx = dy/du × du/dx
A straight line perpendicular to the tangent at a given point on the curve

howToAnswer.instruction: Calculate the derivative using appropriate rules and show clear working
Start from given information and work step-by-step to reach the required result
Use the result from the previous part - do not start from the beginning again

modelAnswer.question: The curve C has equation y = 2x³ - 9x² + 12x + 1. Find the coordinates of the stationary points of C.
modelAnswer.answer: dy/dx = 6x² - 18x + 12. At stationary points, dy/dx = 0. So 6x² - 18x + 12 = 0. Dividing by 6: x² - 3x + 2 = 0. Factoring: (x - 1)(x - 2) = 0. Therefore x = 1 or x = 2. When x = 1: y = 2(1)³ - 9(1)² + 12(1) + 1 = 6. When x = 2: y = 2(8) - 9(4) + 12(2) + 1 = 5. The stationary points are (1, 6) and (2, 5).
markBreakdown: Mark 2: Setting dy/dx = 0 and solving to get x = 1, x = 2
Mark 3: Finding y-coordinate when x = 1 gives y = 6
Mark 4: Finding y-coordinate when x = 2 gives y = 5

commonMistakes: Never write 'dy/dx = 0' without solving the equation - always find the x-values
Students often forget to find both coordinates of stationary points - marks lost for missing y-values
For normal equations, students confuse the gradient with -1/m instead of using the negative reciprocal
When using chain rule, students often forget to multiply by du/dx
Writing tangent equation as y = mx + c instead of y - y₁ = m(x - x₁) loses method marks
examinerTips: Differentiation appears in every Paper 1 - master the basic rules first
Always substitute back to find y-coordinates when finding stationary points
For tangent/normal questions, clearly state the gradient value before writing the equation
Show factoring steps clearly when solving dy/dx = 0 for full method marks
note_data.keyDiagramDescription: A smooth curve showing a tangent line touching at one point and a normal line perpendicular to the tangent at the same point, with stationary points marked where the tangent is horizontal
pastPaperRefs: This topic was tested in M/J 2023 P2 Q7 (6 marks) — they asked students to differentiate f(x) = 3x⁴ - 2x³ + 5x - 1 and find the gradient at x = 2
This topic appeared in O/N 2022 P1 Q12 (4 marks) — students had to find dy/dx when y = (2x + 3)⁵ using the chain rule
This topic was examined in M/J 2022 P2 Q9 (8 marks) — candidates needed to find stationary points of y = x³ - 6x² + 9x + 2 and determine their nature
This topic featured in O/N 2023 P2 Q6 (5 marks) — students were required to differentiate y = x²√x and find where the gradient equals 12

workedExample.question: Find the coordinates of the stationary points of the curve y = 2x³ - 9x² + 12x + 5 and determine the nature of each point. (8 marks)
steps: Step 4: Factorize: (x - 1)(x - 2) = 0, so x = 1 or x = 2
Step 7: Test nature: at x = 1, d²y/dx² = -6 < 0 (maximum); at x = 2, d²y/dx² = 6 > 0 (minimum)
workedExample.answer: Stationary points are (1, 10) which is a maximum and (2, 9) which is a minimum
workedExample.markScheme: 1 mark for correct first derivative, 2 marks for solving dy/dx = 0, 2 marks for correct coordinates, 1 mark for second derivative, 2 marks for correct nature determination

examinerWarnings: Examiner Report 2023: 'Many candidates confused the power rule with the product rule when differentiating terms like x²√x' — always simplify expressions like x²√x = x^(5/2) before differentiating
Examiner Report 2022: 'Candidates frequently made sign errors when applying the chain rule to negative powers' — always write out each step clearly when using the chain rule, especially with brackets

Examiner Report 2023: 'Students often found stationary points correctly but failed to determine their nature' — always use the second derivative test or sign change method to classify maximum/minimum points
note_data.keyDiagram: Students must be able to sketch a curve showing stationary points, labeling maximum points (where the curve has a peak with d²y/dx² < 0) and minimum points (where the curve has a trough with d²y/dx² > 0). The diagram should show the tangent line is horizontal at stationary points (gradient = 0) and include arrows indicating increasing/decreasing regions of the curve.
note_data.quickRecap: Differentiation finds the gradient function dy/dx using the power rule: d/dx(xⁿ) = nxⁿ⁻¹. For stationary points, set dy/dx = 0 and solve for x-values, then find corresponding y-coordinates. Use the second derivative test: d²y/dx² > 0 gives minimum, d²y/dx² < 0 gives maximum. Remember the chain rule for composite functions and always simplify expressions before differentiating.
